bibliographic_data.title: RIT-Eyes: Rendering of near-eye images for eye-tracking applications
bibliographic_data.abstract: Deep neural networks for video-based eye tracking have demonstrated resilience to noisy environments, stray reflections, and low resolution. However, to train these networks, a large number of manually annotated images are required. To alleviate the cumbersome process of manual labeling, computer graphics rendering is employed to automatically generate a large corpus of annotated eye images under various conditions. In this work, we introduce a synthetic eye image generation platform that improves upon previous work by adding features such as an active deformable iris, an aspherical cornea, retinal retro-reflection, gaze-coordinated eyelid deformations, and blinks. To demonstrate the utility of our platform, we render images reflecting the represented gaze distributions inherent in two publicly available datasets, NVGaze and OpenEDS. We also report on the performance of two semantic segmentation architectures (SegNet and RITnet) trained on rendered images and tested on the original datasets.

core_content.novelty_claim: The work introduces a synthetic eye image generation platform that improves upon previous work by adding features such as an active deformable iris, an aspherical cornea, retinal retro-reflection, gaze-coordinated eyelid deformations, and blinks.
core_content.key_methodology: A 3D model of the human eye is used to render 2D imagery similar to what is captured by an eye-tracker. The platform introduces several improvements including an accurate aspherical corneal model, a deformable iris, the lacrimal caruncle, gaze-coordinated blinks, and a retina with retro-reflective properties.
outcomes: The rendered images reflect the represented gaze distributions inherent in two publicly available datasets (NVGaze and OpenEDS).
Two semantic segmentation architectures (SegNet and RITnet) were trained on rendered images and tested on original datasets.
core_content.full_text_markdown: Modern video-based eye-trackers use infrared cameras to monitor movements of the eyes. To train deep neural networks for eye tracking, a large number of manually annotated images are required. This work introduces a synthetic eye image generation platform that improves upon previous work by adding features such as an active deformable iris, an aspherical cornea, retinal retro-reflection, gaze-coordinated eyelid deformations, and blinks. The rendered images reflect the represented gaze distributions inherent in two publicly available datasets (NVGaze and OpenEDS). Two semantic segmentation architectures (SegNet and RITnet) were trained on these rendered images and tested on original datasets.
